_meta.description: Episode × witness alignment matrix for the four major Ancient Near Eastern flood narratives. Curated from Research/Atrahasis.md plus Lambert & Millard 1969, George 2003, Westermann 1984. Excerpts are paraphrased summaries authored for this dataset to avoid copyright concerns with the cited critical editions; consult the named scholarly_anchor for the verbatim translation.
_meta.curator_note: Sumerian Ziusudra cells are flagged philological_uncertainty: partial or fragmentary; the Nippur manuscript (CBS 10673) preserves roughly the lower one-third of the original text in five surviving blocks (lines 37-50, 84-100, 135-162, 195-217, 240-261 per ETCSL 1.7.4). Genesis cells use the consensus scholarly framework — Westermann's Genesis 1-11 commentary for J/P source-critical points where the witnesses overlap or diverge. Sumerian Ziusudra enrichment from Research/Sumerian_Flood_Story.md curator-brief added 2026-05-15.

rows.summary: Humans are made to relieve the gods (or the lesser gods) of labor.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 84-100 (city-founding section; anthropogenesis proper is in lost lines 1-36)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, fragmentary] The five antediluvian cities are established — Eridu, Bad-tibira, Larak, Sippar, Shuruppak — each with its tutelary deity, the cosmic order of the cultivated world is laid out. The specific anthropogenesis narrative is LOST in CBS 10673 (lines 1-36 are missing); only the civilization-founding consequence survives.
sumerian_ziusudra.scholarly_anchor: Civil, in Lambert & Millard 1969, pp.138-145; cf. Peterson 2008 (N 3514, Schøyen) for an additional Old Babylonian fragment expanding the civilization-founding section
divergence_notes: Unique Sumerian feature — the five antediluvian cities are explicitly named (Eridu, Bad-tibira, Larak, Sippar, Shuruppak); Atra-ḫasīs lacks this enumeration
Genesis 4's pre-flood civilization references (Cain's city, the Jubal/Tubal-Cain bronze-and-iron line) are typologically parallel to this section but not directly genealogically related

atrahasis.excerpt: The Igigi do the labor of canals and rivers; after forty years they rebel and burn their tools. The Anunnaki accept Enki's plan: a god (Wē-ila) is slaughtered, his blood and flesh mixed with clay by the mother-goddess Bēlet-ilī. Fourteen portions are gestated; humanity emerges to carry the labor.
atrahasis.scholarly_anchor: Lambert & Millard 1969, pp.56-67 (Old Babylonian recension I.i-iv)
divergence_notes: Distinct anthropogenesis (slain-god blood + clay) absent from Gilgamesh XI and Genesis
Establishes labor-as-purpose framing for humans

rows.divergence_summary: Only Atra-ḫasīs preserves the anthropogenesis frame. The omission in Gilgamesh XI reflects the epic's narrower focus on the Flood as a single retold event; Genesis 1-2's creation accounts precede the flood narrative as separate compositions.

rows.summary: Humans multiply; the noise / weight of human civilization becomes intolerable.

atrahasis.excerpt: The land bellows like a bull; Enlil cannot sleep for the racket. He sends successive plagues — disease, then drought, then famine — each time Enki advises Atra-ḫasīs how to survive. Each crisis partially abates but the population recovers.
divergence_notes: Noise / population-pressure motif is distinctly Mesopotamian
Three escalating non-flood plagues are unique to Atra-ḫasīs

genesis_6_9.excerpt: The LORD sees that the wickedness of humanity is great in the earth and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart is only evil continually. The earth is corrupt and filled with violence.
divergence_notes: Genesis reframes the cause as moral wickedness, not population pressure / noise
No preliminary plagues; the Flood is the first and only divine response

rows.divergence_summary: Atra-ḫasīs grounds divine displeasure in population-pressure / noise (rigmu); Genesis substitutes moral corruption. This is the load-bearing theological re-framing of the Israelite redaction.

rows.summary: A god (or the divine assembly) decides to destroy humanity by flood.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 37-50 (surviving partial block)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, partial] The assembly of An and Enlil decrees that humanity be destroyed by flood. The collective-assembly framing is preserved despite the textual gaps; the surviving lines confirm that the decision is corporate, not unilateral.
divergence_notes: Decision is collective (An + Enlil assembly), as in Atra-ḫasīs and Gilgamesh XI
The pre-decision deliberation (why the flood was decreed — overpopulation? noise? wickedness?) is LOST in CBS 10673; cannot be recovered

atrahasis.excerpt: Enlil escalates from plagues to a final solution. The assembly is convened; the Flood is decreed. The gods swear an oath of secrecy binding none of them to warn humanity.
divergence_notes: Explicit oath-of-secrecy across the divine assembly — load-bearing for the wall-loophole that follows

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: Ut-napishti tells Gilgamesh: the great gods of Shuruppak — Anu, Enlil, Ninurta, Ennugi, and Ea — decided to send the Flood. The decision was sealed in the divine assembly.
divergence_notes: Names the five gods of the assembly explicitly
Compresses Atra-ḫasīs's longer escalation arc into a single decision moment

genesis_6_9.excerpt: And God said unto Noah, The end of all flesh is come before me; for the earth is filled with violence through them; and, behold, I will destroy them with the earth.
divergence_notes: Single deity decides — no assembly
Decision announced directly to Noah without intermediate secrecy frame

rows.divergence_summary: All three Mesopotamian witnesses preserve an assembly-of-gods decision frame; Genesis collapses this to a single divine decree spoken directly to the survivor.

rows.summary: The deity friendly to humanity warns the chosen survivor of the coming Flood.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 135-162 (the wall-loophole block)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, partial] Enki speaks at the side of a wall; Ziusudra stands beside the wall and hears the warning to build a great boat. The wall-loophole motif is preserved in essentially the same form as in Atra-ḫasīs III.i.11-50 and Gilgamesh XI.20-31 — establishing this as the EARLIEST surviving attestation of one of the most distinctive Mesopotamian theological motifs.
divergence_notes: EARLIEST attestation of the wall-loophole motif — pre-Akkadian, establishing the motif as foundational rather than an Atra-ḫasīs innovation
Same Enki-speaks-to-wall mechanism as Atra-ḫasīs and Gilgamesh XI; the Sumerian original confirms the loophole is not a later Akkadian addition

atrahasis.excerpt: Enki addresses the reed wall of Atra-ḫasīs's hut: 'Wall, listen to me! Reed hut, pay attention to my words! Demolish your house, build a ship; abandon possessions, save life; bring into the ship the seed of all living things.' Atra-ḫasīs overhears.
divergence_notes: Enki addresses the wall, not Atra-ḫasīs directly — preserves the secrecy oath formally while subverting it functionally
The legalism is the Mesopotamian theological signature of Enki's character

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: Ea speaks through the reed hut, addressing it: 'Reed hut, reed hut! Wall, wall! Reed hut, listen; wall, attend! O man of Shuruppak, son of Ubar-Tutu, demolish your house, build a boat; abandon possessions, save life; bring into the boat the seed of all living things.'
divergence_notes: Verbatim transmission of the Atra-ḫasīs scene with Ut-napishti named
Demonstrates the SB redactor's direct dependence on the OB Atra-ḫasīs

genesis_6_9.excerpt: God speaks directly to Noah, giving the ark's specifications and the duration of the Flood. No intermediary, no wall, no secrecy frame.
divergence_notes: Direct divine address replaces the wall-loophole
Removes the legalism — the Israelite God does not need to subvert a divine-assembly oath
Westermann reads this as the Priestly redactor's de-mythologization of the inherited narrative

rows.divergence_summary: All three Mesopotamian witnesses preserve the speech-through-the-wall loophole — across Sumerian, Old Babylonian Akkadian, and Standard Babylonian Akkadian. Genesis removes it. This is one of the cleanest single-detail Mesopotamian → Hebrew theological transformations in the comparative-religion corpus.

rows.summary: The survivor builds a vessel to divine specifications and loads it.

atrahasis.excerpt: Atra-ḫasīs builds a great boat sealed with bitumen. He loads family, animals, possessions, and provisions. The text describes its decks and structure.
divergence_notes: Ark is rectangular/multi-deck in the Atra-ḫasīs description

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: Ut-napishti builds a cube-shaped boat: 'Its width and length shall be the same.' Six decks, divided into nine compartments per deck. He loads family, craftsmen, animals, and provisions. The construction takes seven days.
divergence_notes: Cube specification is distinctive to Gilgamesh — Atra-ḫasīs description does not give a cube
Adds craftsmen to the manifest

genesis_6_9.excerpt: The ark is to be built of gopher wood, three hundred cubits long, fifty cubits broad, thirty cubits high, three stories, one window, one door. Pitched with pitch inside and out. Noah loads his family, two of every kind of unclean animal, seven of every clean (J source) or two of every kind (P source).
divergence_notes: Distinct rectangular proportions (6:1:0.6 ratio) — not the cubic Gilgamesh form nor the rectangular Atra-ḫasīs form
J/P source-critical issue: the animal-counts differ between Gen 7:2 (J) and Gen 6:19-20 (P)

rows.divergence_summary: Atra-ḫasīs and Genesis both give rectangular ark forms; Gilgamesh XI uniquely specifies a cube. The diverging specifications suggest each tradition independently inherited the ark-construction motif without normalizing its dimensions.

rows.summary: The Flood comes; humanity is destroyed except for those on the ark.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 195-217 (the flood proper)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, partial] The flood rages for seven days and seven nights; the wind howls across the land. The seven-and-seven duration matches Atra-ḫasīs (III.iv.25) and Gilgamesh XI (six days and seven nights — a one-day variant in the Standard Babylonian recension). The seven-and-seven figure is thus the canonical Mesopotamian flood-duration across all three witnesses, and Genesis's 40-day rain + 150-day prevailing-waters scheme is a substantial Hebrew innovation.
divergence_notes: Seven days and seven nights — converges with Atra-ḫasīs; close to Gilgamesh XI's six-and-seven; sharply distinct from Genesis 7:12 (40 days) and 7:24 (150 days)
Establishes the Mesopotamian seven-and-seven as cross-period canonical

atrahasis.excerpt: The Flood comes; the Anunnaki retreat to the heaven of Anu in fear of their own flood; Bēlet-ilī weeps over what is being destroyed. The waters rage for seven days and seven nights.
divergence_notes: Gods themselves are afraid of the flood — distinct mythological texture absent in Genesis
Bēlet-ilī's lament is the theological soft spot of the Mesopotamian narrative

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: The storm-god Adad rages, the Anunnaki raise their torches, the heavens go dark; even the gods cower like dogs at a wall. The Flood lasts six days and seven nights.
divergence_notes: Six days and seven nights — a one-day variant on the Atra-ḫasīs seven and seven
Vivid storm-god imagery characteristic of the SB redaction

genesis_6_9.excerpt: The fountains of the great deep are broken up and the windows of heaven are opened. The rain is on the earth forty days and forty nights. The waters prevail upon the earth one hundred and fifty days.
divergence_notes: Forty days and nights of rain (vs Mesopotamian seven)
150 days of prevailing waters — a much longer total duration
J/P source-critical issue: the durations are inconsistent between Gen 7:12 (J: 40 days) and 7:24 (P: 150 days)

rows.divergence_summary: Flood duration is one of the cleanest distinctions across the witnesses: Sumerian/OB/SB Akkadian all converge on roughly seven days; Genesis extends to forty days (rain) or 150 days (prevailing waters). The Israelite extension may reflect the Priestly source's interest in calendrical exactitude.

rows.summary: The ark grounds on a mountain; the survivor sends out birds to test for dry land.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story (gap at ~lines 218-239; CBS 10673 lost)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [absent] The landfall episode is NOT preserved in the Sumerian flood story. The textual gap between the flood-proper (lines 195-217) and the sacrifice block (lines 240+) covers what would have been the landfall episode. The bird-release motif found in Gilgamesh XI and Genesis 8 is therefore NOT confirmed for the Sumerian layer — it may be a Gilgamesh-recension innovation, or it may have been present in the lost Sumerian section.
sumerian_ziusudra.scholarly_anchor: Civil, in Lambert & Millard 1969, pp.144-145 (gap-analysis only)
divergence_notes: Bird-release motif's earliest preserved attestation is Gilgamesh XI (Standard Babylonian, c. 1200 BCE); the Sumerian witness cannot confirm or deny pre-Akkadian presence
If the bird-release IS originally Gilgamesh-recension, Genesis's olive-leaf-and-dove tradition derives from the Gilgamesh layer specifically, not from the broader pre-Akkadian flood tradition

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: The boat grounds on Mount Nimush. After seven days Ut-napishti sends out a dove, which returns. Then a swallow, which returns. Then a raven, which does not return — the waters have receded. He opens the boat.
divergence_notes: Mount Nimush (= Mount Niṣir in older translations); identified with modern Pira Magrun in Iraqi Kurdistan
Three birds in order: dove, swallow, raven
Raven goes last — opposite Genesis order

genesis_6_9.excerpt: The ark rests upon the mountains of Ararat in the seventh month, seventeenth day. Noah sends out a raven first; then a dove three times, the second returning with an olive leaf, the third not returning.
divergence_notes: Mountains of Ararat (region, not specific peak)
Two birds (raven first, then three releases of dove) — distinct order from Gilgamesh
Olive leaf is unique to Genesis

rows.divergence_summary: The bird-release motif is shared with Gilgamesh XI but the species and order differ. Atra-ḫasīs is too fragmentary at this point to confirm whether the motif is OB or originated in the SB redaction.

rows.summary: The survivor offers sacrifice; the gods (or God) respond.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 240-250 (the sacrifice + Utu epiphany block)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, partial] Ziusudra opens the great boat and sees Utu the sun-god, who has risen and flooded the world with light. He prostrates himself before Utu, slaughters an ox and a sheep as sacrifice before An and Enlil. The SUN-GOD UTU EPIPHANY at this episode is UNIQUE to the Sumerian witness — neither Atra-ḫasīs nor Gilgamesh XI preserves a sun-god appearance at the sacrifice scene.
divergence_notes: UNIQUE to Sumerian: the sun-god Utu's epiphany after the flood — possibly the visual marker of the cosmic order being restored
Atra-ḫasīs and Gilgamesh XI both have the post-flood sacrifice ('gods like flies' image) but neither preserves Utu's appearance

Genesis 8:22 ('seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night shall not cease') is a partial theological parallel: the sun's order is restored after the flood, framed as covenantal not epiphanic

atrahasis.excerpt: Atra-ḫasīs offers sacrifice. The gods — who have been starving without humans to feed them — gather around the smoke 'like flies.' Enlil arrives, sees the survivors, is furious; Enki defends the loophole he exploited.
divergence_notes: The 'gods like flies' image is one of the most-cited Atra-ḫasīs phrases
Theological logic: no humans = no sacrifices = hungry gods

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: Ut-napishti pours out a libation on the mountain peak; sets out seven and seven incense vessels. The gods smell the savor, the gods gather like flies around the sacrificer.
divergence_notes: Preserves the 'gods like flies' image from Atra-ḫasīs
Adds the libation-and-incense detail

genesis_6_9.excerpt: Noah builds an altar and offers burnt offerings of every clean beast and every clean bird. The LORD smells the sweet savor and resolves in his heart never again to curse the ground for man's sake.
divergence_notes: Preserves the smell-of-the-sacrifice motif — anthropomorphism striking in the Israelite context
Removes the 'gods like flies' image and the hunger framing
Resolution: divine self-restraint, not collective post-mortem

rows.divergence_summary: All four witnesses preserve the sacrifice-and-divine-response motif. The 'gods like flies' image survives across the Mesopotamian witnesses but is excised in Genesis; the underlying anthropomorphism (deity smells the sacrifice) survives in Hebrew.

rows.summary: A new theological order is established to prevent recurrence.

sumerian_ziusudra.citation: Sumerian Flood Story lines 251-261 (the eternal-life-in-Dilmun resolution)
sumerian_ziusudra.excerpt: [paraphrase, partial] An and Enlil grant Ziusudra *nam-tilla* ('life like a god's,' eternal life) and settle him in DILMUN, 'where the sun rises' — the paradisal land at the eastern edge of the cosmos. This Dilmun-specific resolution is DISTINCTIVE TO THE SUMERIAN witness: Atra-ḫasīs has the birth-control-by-theological-design framework; Gilgamesh XI relocates Ut-napishti to 'the mouth of the rivers' (a vaguer location); only the Sumerian places the survivor specifically in Dilmun.
divergence_notes: Ziusudra alone receives explicit eternal life (*nam-tilla*) — different resolution from Atra-ḫasīs's birth-control-by-theological-design framework
DILMUN specifically — the paradisal eastern land also mentioned in *Enki and Ninhursag* — is the unique Sumerian setting; not Gilgamesh XI's 'mouth of the rivers'

The sun-rising-eastern-paradise framework has been suggested as a possible influence on Genesis 2's Eden 'in the east' (Genesis 2:8), though the connection is speculative and not load-bearing for current scholarship

atrahasis.excerpt: The assembly establishes a new order: mortality, miscarriage, and infertility are introduced as built-in population controls. A class of celibate priestesses (ugbabtu, etc.) is established. The demon Pašittu takes some newborns. Humans remain; the conditions for human existence are tighter.
divergence_notes: Birth-control-by-theological-design — the most distinctive Atra-ḫasīs feature
Inverse of Genesis's covenant-of-restraint

gilgamesh_xi.excerpt: Enlil enters the boat, takes Ut-napishti and his wife by the hand, and grants them immortality, settling them far away at the mouth of the rivers — like Ziusudra in the Sumerian. The new theological order is not elaborated.
divergence_notes: Adopts the Ziusudra immortality-resolution rather than Atra-ḫasīs's birth-control framework
Three-layer dialogue between the witnesses on this point

genesis_6_9.excerpt: God resolves never again to curse the ground or destroy every living creature. Be fruitful and multiply (encouraging the very population growth that triggered the Flood in Mesopotamian thought). Establishes the rainbow covenant — the sign of divine self-binding. Permits eating of animals but forbids bloodshed.
divergence_notes: Covenant-of-restraint — God binds Himself, not humanity
Be-fruitful-and-multiply is theologically the opposite of Atra-ḫasīs's population-control resolution
Rainbow sign is the visible marker of the new order

rows.divergence_summary: The post-flood theological resolution is where the four witnesses diverge most dramatically. Atra-ḫasīs: built-in population control. Sumerian / Gilgamesh: immortality for the survivor. Genesis: divine self-restraint + encouraged human fertility. The Genesis resolution is theologically the most inverted from the Atra-ḫasīs source.
